root \ about \ projects + writings \ academic website

linear transformations, I

The considerations in this post may best be described as follows: Suppose that we consider a linear transformation

\begin{equation} \label{begin-eqn} \phi: \bbr^2{}_2 \to \bbr^2{}_2, \end{equation}

where we recall

\begin{equation}\notag \bbr^2{}_2 \overset{\text{def}}{=} \bbr^2 \otimes (\bbr^2)^\ast. \end{equation}

Let $e_1,e_2$ be the standard ordered basis of $\bbr^2$ and $\eta^1,\eta^2$ the associated dual basis of the dual space. Then the tensor product $\bbr^2{}_2$ is $4$-dimensional, with basis given by the tensors

\begin{equation} \label{ordered-eqn} e_1 \otimes \eta^1, \ e_1 \otimes \eta^2, \ e_2 \otimes \eta^1, \ e_2 \otimes \eta^2. \end{equation}

As we learned in linear algebra, it follows that $\phi$ may be represented by a $4\times 4$ matrix with respect to the basis \eqref{ordered-eqn}. However, the particular linear ordering that I chose for \eqref{ordered-eqn} was completely arbitrary; had I chose to order the tensors as, say,

\begin{equation}\notag e_2 \otimes \eta^2, \ e_1 \otimes \eta^2, \ e_2 \otimes \eta^1, \ e_1 \otimes \eta^1, \end{equation}

we would obtain a different representative matrix. The point is, that there is no natural linear ordering of basis tensors. As a corollary, we see that the “flattening layers” used in neural networks are unnatural.

Let me be a bit more precise.

While the word natural does have a particular, technical definition in mathematics, roughly, to say that some process is natural means that it is free of arbitrary choices. As an example of something that is unnatural, when we fix ordered bases of the domain and codomain of a linear transformation, the representative matrix is unnaturally associated with the linear transformation, precisely because we had to make arbitrary choices for the bases. So, no matter how we represent the transformation \eqref{begin-eqn}, whether it be by using matrices or tensors or whatever, the representation will always be unnatural if it depends on the choice of bases. However, in the case of representing the particular transformation \eqref{begin-eqn} using matrices, there are two sources of unnaturality, the first arising from the arbitrary choice of ordered bases of $\bbr^2$ and $(\bbr^2)^\ast$, and the second from arbitrarily choosing the linear ordering of the basis tensors \eqref{ordered-eqn}.

My goal in this post is to show you how we can cut out the second source of unnaturality, by representing linear transformations like \eqref{begin-eqn} via mixed tensors.

As we will see later, applied to feed-forward neural networks, this scheme of representing linear transformations by general tensors, instead of matrices, will clarify, unify, and simplify the mathematics. For example, in many convolutional neural network (CNN) architectures, the network has both a convolutional part and a fully-connected part, the two being connected by an intermediate (and unnatural) “flattening layer.” But the mathematical description of the two parts of the network, at least in introductory texts, seem to have little in common: the affine preactivation functions in the fully-connected part are easily described as multiplication by a matrix followed by addition of a bias term, while the convolutions do not appear to have any such description, which complicates any attempt to mathematically treat the whole network as a single, cohesive object. However, if we represent all preactivation functions using tensors (in short, if everything becomes a tensor), then the relationship between the convolutional layers and the fully-connected layers becomes much clearer.

But even focusing on networks that are entirely fully-connected, the emphasis on tensors over matrices has a clarifying and simplifying effect, which we will see when we come to the backpropagation algorithm.

table of contents

prerequisites

linear transformations and tensor products

Let’s begin with a simple example to help orient ourselves. Suppose that $V_1,V_2,V_3,U_1,U_2$ are all finite-dimensional vector spaces, and suppose that

\begin{equation} \phi: V_1 \otimes V_2^\ast \otimes V_3^\ast \to U_1^\ast \otimes U_2 \end{equation}

is a linear transformation. Thus, $\phi$ maps mixed tensors of type $\binom{1}{2}$ to mixed tensors of type $\binom{1}{1}$. If we fix bases, then a tensor

\begin{equation}\notag X^a{}_{bc} = X^\alpha{}_{\beta\gamma} \ e_\alpha \otimes \eta^\beta \otimes \eta^\gamma \end{equation}

in the domain of $\phi$ may be expanded as a linear combination of basis tensors, with summation over repeated indices implied, as always. By linearity, we get that

\begin{equation} \label{backward-eqn} \phi(X^a{}_{bc}) = X^\alpha{}_{\beta\gamma} \ \phi(e_\alpha \otimes \eta^\beta \otimes \eta^\gamma). \end{equation}

Now, for each $\alpha, \beta,\gamma$, the tensor $\phi(e_\alpha \otimes \eta^\beta \otimes \eta^\gamma)$ may be expanded as a linear combination of basis tensors as

\begin{equation}\notag \phi(e_\alpha \otimes \eta^\beta \otimes \eta^\gamma) = W_\alpha{}^{\beta\gamma}{}_\delta{}^\epsilon \ \eta^\delta \otimes e_\epsilon, \end{equation}

for some collection of (unique!) real numbers $W_\alpha{}^{\beta\gamma}{}_\delta{}^\epsilon$. If we substitute these equations back in \eqref{backward-eqn}, we get

\begin{equation} \label{final-eqn} \phi(X^a{}_{bc}) = X^\alpha{}_{\beta\gamma} W_\alpha{}^{\beta\gamma}{}_\delta{}^\epsilon \ \eta^\delta \otimes e_\epsilon. \end{equation}

Now, we may define an abstract tensor by setting

\begin{equation}\notag W_a{}^{bc}{}_d{}^e = W_\alpha{}^{\beta\gamma}{}_\delta{}^\epsilon \ \eta^\alpha \otimes e_\beta \otimes e_\gamma \otimes \eta^\delta \otimes e_\epsilon \in \underbrace{V_1^\ast \otimes V_2 \otimes V_3}_{\text{dual of domain of $\phi$}} \otimes \underbrace{U_1^\ast \otimes U_2}_{\text{codomain of $\phi$}}. \end{equation}

Observe that \eqref{final-eqn} is nothing but

\begin{equation} \label{clock-eqn} \phi(X^a{}_{bc})_d{}^e = X^a{}_{bc}W_a{}^{bc}{}_d{}^e, \end{equation}

where the right-hand side is given by the tensor product of $X$ and $W$, followed by contraction along three indices. This particular type of product is given a name:

Definition. Let $X$ and $W$ be two mixed tensors of ranks $m$ and $n$, respectively. The dot product of $X$ and $W$, denoted $X\cdot W$, is obtained as follows:

  1. If $m\geq n$, we take the tensor product $XW$ and contract all $n$ indices of $W$ in order along the last $n$ indices of $X$ (if defined).
  2. If $m <n$, we take the tensor product $XW$ and contract all $m$ indices of $X$ in order along the first $m$ indices of $W$ (if defined).

What I mean by the parenthetical statement “if defined” is easily explained with attempting to form the dot product $v^a \cdot f_a$ between a vector $v^a\in V$ and a covector $f_a\in W^\ast$: this dot product is only defined if $V=W$.

In view of this definition, equation \eqref{clock-eqn} may be rewritten concisely as

\begin{equation}\notag \phi(X) = X \cdot W. \end{equation}

In this situation, $W$ is called the weight tensor with respect to the fixed choice of bases. This tensor represents $\phi$, in that $\phi$ is nothing but a dot product against the weight tensor.

Though I explained this scheme in the specific case of a fixed linear transformation $\phi$, I hope you can see how these considerations generalize to any linear transformation $\phi$ between two spaces of mixed tensors. For example, with respect to fixed, ordered bases, the weight tensor of a linear transformation

\begin{equation}\notag \phi: V_1 \otimes V_2 \to U_1^\ast \otimes U_2^\ast \otimes U_3^\ast \end{equation}

would be a tensor in

\begin{equation}\notag W_{ab}{}^{cde} \in \underbrace{(V_1)^\ast \otimes (V_2)^\ast}_{\text{dual of domain of $\phi$}} \otimes \underbrace{U_1^\ast \otimes U_2^\ast \otimes U_3^\ast}_{\text{codomain of $\phi$}}. \end{equation}

We thus have:

Theorem. Any linear transformation between two spaces of mixed tensors may be represented, with respect to a fixed choice of ordered bases, as a dot product against a weight tensor.

Before moving on to the next section, let’s do a quick sanity check. According to our discussion, a linear transformation of the form

\begin{equation}\notag \phi: \bbr_2 \to \bbr_3 \end{equation}

is supposed to be represented (with respect to a choice of ordered bases) by a weight tensor

\begin{equation}\notag W^{a}{}_b \in \bbr^{2}{}_3 = \bbr^2 \otimes \bbr_3. \end{equation}

Suppose that

\begin{equation}\notag \phi(\eta^1) = W^1{}_1\eta^1 + W^1{}_2\eta^2 + W^1{}_3\eta^3 \quad \text{and} \quad \phi(\eta^2) = W^2{}_1\eta^1 + W^2{}_2 \eta^2 + W^2{}_3\eta^3, \end{equation}

where $\eta^1, \eta^2$ is the standard ordered (dual) basis of $\bbr_2$ and $\eta^1,\eta^2,\eta^3$ is the standard ordered (dual) basis of $\bbr_3$. The weight tensor is then given by

\begin{equation}\notag W^a{}_b = W^\alpha{}_\beta \ e_\alpha \otimes \eta^\beta, \end{equation}

where $e_1,e_2$ is the standard ordered basis of $\bbr^2$. Thus, for a given covector $f\in \bbr_2$, we have

\begin{equation}\notag \phi(f) = f\cdot W = f_\alpha W^\alpha{}_\beta \ \eta^\beta = f_\alpha W^\alpha{}_1 \ \eta^1 + f_\alpha W^\alpha{}_2 \ \eta^2 + f_\alpha W^\alpha{}_3 \ \eta^3, \end{equation}

which replicates the expression in the more familiar matrix representation of $\phi$:

\begin{equation}\notag \phi \left( \begin{bmatrix} f_1 & f_2 \end{bmatrix}\right) = \begin{bmatrix} f_1 & f_2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} W^1{}_1 & W^1{}_2 & W^1{}_3 \\ W^2{}_1 & W^2{}_2 & W^2{}_3 \end{bmatrix}. \end{equation}

compositions

linear transformations and direct sums

Let’s consider the special case of a linear transformation

\begin{equation} \phi: \bbr^2 \otimes \bbr^3 \to \bbr^3 \otimes \bbr^2. \end{equation}

the direct sum existence theorem

Recall way back in the first post, that I motivated the binary tensor product $\otimes$ by “inventing” it as a way to multiply vectors $v\in V$ and $w\in W$, which may come from different vector spaces. To make this work, a third vector space $V \otimes W$ was invented that serves as the target for this new multiplication, in the sense that the binary tensor product of vectors defines a map

\begin{equation} \label{times-map-eqn} V \times W \to V \otimes W, \quad (v,w) \mapsto v \otimes w. \end{equation}

Moreover, this new invented product, $\otimes$, has several natural properties encoded in the bilinearity relations. But what if we wanted to add the vectors $v$ and $w$ instead? Could we “invent” a way to do this, in the same spirit as our invention of the tensor product?

Yes!

Suppose that some sort of hypothetical addition of $v$ and $w$ exists, which we denote $v \oplus w$, and call the direct sum of $v$ and $w$. What sort of properties would we want it to have?

First, we need to decide what sort of vector the direct sum $v\oplus w$ is. Where does it live? Back in $V$ or $W$? As I just mentioned, this was the exact problem that we faced when considering the tensor product $v\otimes w$ (where does it live?), and to resolve it, we invented the new vector space $V \otimes W$ to contain these products. We shall do the same thing here, by inventing a new vector space $V \oplus W$, which we call the direct sum of $V$ and $W$, in order to obtain an “addition” map

\begin{equation} \label{addition-map-eqn} V \times W \to V \oplus W, \quad (v,w) \mapsto v \oplus w, \end{equation}

in complete analogy with \eqref{times-map-eqn}.

The properties that we shall require of our new direct sum can be encoded in the equations

\begin{equation} \label{defining-eqn} (v+v’) \oplus (w + w’) = (v\oplus w) + (v’ \oplus w’) \end{equation}

and

\begin{equation} \label{defining2-eqn} (cv) \oplus (cw) = c(v\oplus w), \end{equation}

for all $v,v’ \in V$, $w,w’\in W$, and $c\in \bbr$. Take care to note that the single symbol $+$ occurs three times in the equation \eqref{defining-eqn}, and it stands for three different additions. Indeed, the addition $v+v’$ on the left-hand side is happening in $V$, the addition $w+w’$ is happening in $W$, while the addition on the right-hand side of the equation is the vector addition in $V\oplus W$. The equations \eqref{defining-eqn} roughly say that these three additions interact coherently, and not in some random, haphazard fashion. The second set of defining equations \eqref{defining2-eqn} have a similar interpretation: notice that the scalings $cv$ and $cw$ are happening in $V$ and $W$, respectively, while the scaling on the right-hand side is happening in $V\oplus W$. Thus, these equations say that these three different scalar multiplications interact coherently.

The relations \eqref{defining-eqn} and \eqref{defining2-eqn} are the “additive analogs” of the bilinearity relations for the tensor product, in the sense that they completely characterize the direct sum, just as the bilinearity relations completely characterized the tensor product. When we discussed the tensor product, we deduced the bilinearity relations by assuming that the tensor product exists, and then writing down the natural properties that it ought to have. However, we still needed to appeal to an existence theorem in order to conclude that the tensor product exists! The situation here with the direct sum is similar: though we have deduced the relations \eqref{defining-eqn} and \eqref{defining2-eqn} as properties that the direct sum ought to have, we haven’t actually shown that the direct sum exists. However, in complete analogy with the Tensor Existence Theorem, we have:

Direct Sum Existence Theorem. Both the vector space $V \oplus W$ and the addition $\oplus$ exist. More precisely, the map \eqref{addition-map-eqn} exists, and it has the following properties:

  1. It is surjective.
  2. The relations \eqref{defining-eqn} and \eqref{defining2-eqn} hold.
  3. No other relations besides \eqref{defining-eqn} and \eqref{defining2-eqn} hold.

As a consequence, the addition map \eqref{addition-map-eqn} is an isomorphism of vector spaces.

Note that the Direct Sum Existence Theorem says more about direct sums than the Tensor Existence Theorem says about tensor products. Indeed, the latter simply says that tensor products exist, but it doesn’t give you an explicit construction for the tensor product (you’d have to look for it in the proof). In contrast, the Direct Sum Existence Theorem gives you an explicit model for the direct sum, namely the cartesian product $V\times W$. Thus, you would be quite correct to identify $V\oplus W$ with $V\times W$, and this is the definition often given for $V\oplus W$ in many sources.

You might ask: why didn’t I simply define the direct sum $V\oplus W$ as $V\times W$, and avoid this extended discussion?

My goal is to shift the emphasis from any particular model of the direct sum, like the cartesian product, to what’s really important: the relations \eqref{defining-eqn} and \eqref{defining2-eqn}. The whole point of the direct sum is that it allows us to add vectors that live in different vector spaces, and it does so in as “natural” a way as possible, without any extraneous properties or relations. We want to cook up a way to make sense of $v\oplus w$, but we don’t particularly care exactly how $v\oplus w$ is constructed, just as long as it has the natural properties that we want (and no others).

Taken a step further, there’s a school of thought in theoretical mathematics that systematically de-emphasizes static, model-level properties and constructions of objects in favor of defining and manipulating objects via their dynamic, “morphism-level” properties. In other words, what’s important about a mathematical object is not the particularities and specificities of any one of its constructions, but rather how it interacts with all the other objects in its category. This is (one of) the main lessons in category theory.

So, to summarize, while my approach to the direct sum is not exactly the one taken in category theory—for that, I would have had to explain biprodcuts—it is still categorical in spirit, since it shifts attention away from the construction of $V\oplus W$ as a cartesian product in favor of the relations \eqref{defining-eqn} and \eqref{defining2-eqn}.

properties of the direct sum

The vector spaces $V$ and $W$ may be embedded in the direct sum $V\oplus W$, allowing us to view a vector $v$ as either living in $V$, or the direct sum $V\oplus W$. Indeed, to accomplish this, we agree to write

\begin{equation} \label{convention-eqn} v = v \oplus 0_W \quad \text{and} \quad w = 0_V \oplus w, \end{equation}

where $0_V$ and $0_W$ are the zero vectors (i.e., the additive identities) in $V$ and $W$. Be sure to understand that these equations are not literally true, and are merely conventions that we are agreeing upon! Using these identifications, and by taking $w=w’=0_W$ in \eqref{defining-eqn}, we get that

\begin{equation} \label{v-eqn} v + v’ = v+v’. \end{equation}

This equation appears to have no content, but remember that the addition on the left-hand side is in $V$, while the addition on the right-hand side is in $V\oplus W$. Similarly, by taking $v=v’=0_V$ in \eqref{defining-eqn}, we get

\begin{equation} \label{w-eqn} w + w’ = w+w’. \end{equation}

Taken together, these last two displayed equations show that the addition in $V\oplus W$ extends the pre-existing additions in $V$ and $W$. A third interesting consequence of the conventions \eqref{convention-eqn} is that, if we take $v’=0_V$ and $w’ = 0_W$ in \eqref{defining-eqn}, we get

\begin{equation} \label{oplus-eqn} v \oplus w = v+ w. \end{equation}

This shows that the direct sum operation $\oplus$ may be identified with the vector space addition in $V\oplus W$.

Essentially, all of these identifications justify us writing strings of additions of vectors in $V$ and $W$ like

\begin{equation} \label{string-eqn} v_1 + w_1 + v_2 + v_3 + w_2 + w_3 \in V\oplus W, \end{equation}

where the $v$’s are in $V$ and the $w$’s are in $W$. The identifications \eqref{oplus-eqn} tell us that, in this string, whenever we see an addition like $v_\ast + w_\ast$, that that’s really the direct sum $v_\ast \oplus w_\ast$; the identifications \eqref{v-eqn} tell us that, when we see something like $v_\ast + v_{\ast}$, with two vectors in $v$ being added, that that’s really an addition in $V$; and finally, whenever we see $w_\ast + w_\ast$, the convention \eqref{w-eqn} tells us that that’s really an addition in $W$. The moral is that strings like \eqref{string-eqn} are written all the time, and that if you dig deep enough, you can make precise sense out of them; however, I encourage you to take strings like this at face value, and manipulate them just like you would any other sum, and only dig deeper into their meaning if you are absolutely forced to!

direct sums as arrays of vector spaces; abstract indices, again

We could, if we really wanted to, go through a careful construction of direct sums of more than two vectors spaces. Such things would look like

\begin{equation}\notag V^1 \oplus V^2 \oplus \cdots \oplus V^n. \end{equation}

However, at the end of the day, we think of this $n$-fold direct sum as consisting of nothing but strings of additions like \eqref{string-eqn} of vectors from the $V_i$’s. For example, inside the $3$-fold direct sum

\begin{equation}\notag V \oplus W \oplus U \end{equation}

I could write a string like

\begin{equation}\notag v_1 + v_2 + u_1 + v_3 + w_1 + u_2 + w_2, \end{equation}

and if I really wanted to, I could carefully take it apart and say precisely what each of the additions really mean. But we won’t do this! Instead, we will just assume that strings like this make sense, with the knowledge that we could carefully explain them if we were forced to.

Now, though I have deliberately de-emphasized the fact that the direct sum $V\oplus W$ may be modeled as the cartesian product $V\times W$, one consequence of this model is that vectors in $V\oplus W$ may be represented as column (or row) vectors:

\begin{equation}\notag v+w \leftrightarrow \begin{bmatrix} v \\ w \end{bmatrix}, \quad v_1+w+v_2 \leftrightarrow \begin{bmatrix} v_1 + v_2 \\ w \end{bmatrix}, \quad \text{etc.} \end{equation}

Generalizing, if we are given three vectors spaces, say $V_1,V_2,V_3$, then vectors in the $3$-fold direct sum $V_1 \oplus V_2 \oplus V_3$ may be represented as vectors as well:

\begin{equation}\notag v_1+v_2+v_3 \leftrightarrow \begin{bmatrix} v_1 \\ v_2 \\ v_3 \end{bmatrix}. \end{equation}

Representing vectors in direct sums as column vectors is quite natural, as long as the indexing of the direct sum is linear, like this:

\begin{equation}\notag \bigoplus_{\alpha=1}^n V_\alpha = V_1 \oplus V_2 \oplus \cdots \oplus V_n. \end{equation}

But we could just as well index a direct sum non-linearly, using multiple indices, like this:

\begin{equation}\notag \bigoplus_{\alpha=1}^{2}\bigoplus_{\beta=1}^2 V_{\alpha\beta} = V_{1 \ 1} \oplus V_{1 \ 2} \oplus V_{2 \ 1} \oplus V_{2 \ 2}. \end{equation}

In this case, it’s more natural to write the vectors in the direct sum as $2\times 2$ arrays:

\begin{equation}\notag v_{1 \ 1} + v_{1 \ 2} + v_{2 \ 1} + v_{2 \ 2} \leftrightarrow \begin{bmatrix} v_{1 \ 1} & v_{1 \ 2} \\ v_{2 \ 1} & v_{2 \ 2}\end{bmatrix}. \end{equation}

You can see what I’m getting at, right? If we had, say, a direct sum of the form

\begin{equation} \label{abstract-ind-eqn} \bigoplus_{\rho=1}^r \bigoplus_{\sigma=1}^s \bigoplus_{\tau=1}^t V^{\rho\sigma\tau}, \end{equation}

then we could represent its vectors as triply-indexed arrays of size $r \times s \times t$.

More generally, we set

\begin{equation} \label{indices-use-eqn} V^{r_1r_2\ldots r_n} \overset{\text{def}}{=} \bigoplus_{\rho_1=1}^{r_1} \bigoplus_{\rho_2=1}^{r_2} \cdots \bigoplus_{\rho_n=1}^{r_1} V^{\rho_1\rho_2\ldots \rho_n}. \end{equation}

Vectors in this last direct sum can be represented as $n$-fold indexed arrays of size $r_1\times r_2 \times \cdots \times r_n$.

Recall, however, that we’ve already encountered notation like this in the form of

\begin{equation} \label{old-eqn} \bbr^{2 \ 2} \overset{\text{def}}{=} \bbr^2 \otimes \bbr^2, \end{equation}

for example. Is this usage of indices consistent with \eqref{indices-use-eqn}? To investigate, we note that

\begin{equation}\notag \bbr^{2 \ 2} = \bbr^2 \otimes \bbr^2 = \left( \bigoplus_{\rho=1}^2 \bbr \right) \otimes \left( \bigoplus_{\sigma=1}^2 \bbr \right) = \underbrace{\bbr}_{\rho=1, \ \sigma=1} \oplus \underbrace{\bbr}_{\rho=1, \ \sigma=2} \oplus \underbrace{\bbr}_{\rho=2, \ \sigma=1} \oplus \underbrace{\bbr,}_{\rho=2, \ \sigma=2} \end{equation}

and the right-hand side is exactly $\bbr^{2 \ 2}$, according to \eqref{indices-use-eqn}.
